skill.name: gcp-cloud-run
skill.description: Specialized skill for building production-ready serverless applications on Google Cloud Platform. Covers Cloud Run services, Cloud Run Functions, cold start optimization, and event-driven architectures.
skill.summary: Deploy and optimize serverless containerized applications on GCP Cloud Run with best practices for performance and cost.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer detected 43 potential issues but all are false positives. The SKILL.md file contains documentation and code examples (Dockerfiles, JavaScript, YAML, bash) for GCP Cloud Run deployment patterns, not executable code. External command detections are gcloud CLI examples in markdown code blocks. Environment variable access is standard Node.js PORT configuration for Cloud Run. No actual security risks present.

content.user_title: Deploy serverless applications on GCP Cloud Run
content.value_statement: Building production-ready serverless apps on GCP is complex with many configuration options. This skill provides proven patterns for Cloud Run services, Functions, cold start optimization, and event-driven architectures.
actual_capabilities: Deploy containerized services to Cloud Run with multi-stage Docker builds
Create event-driven Cloud Run Functions for Pub/Sub and Cloud Storage triggers
Implement cold start optimization with CPU boost, minimum instances, and lazy loading
Configure Cloud Build pipelines for automated deployment
Apply security best practices including non-root containers and distroless images
Optimize memory, CPU, and concurrency settings for cost and performance
limitations: Focuses on Node.js runtime examples; other languages require adaptation
Does not cover GKE Autopilot or Compute Engine alternatives
Examples assume Google Cloud SDK is configured with proper credentials
Does not include infrastructure-as-code templates (Terraform, Pulumi)

use_cases.title: Web API Deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy a Node.js Express API to Cloud Run with automatic scaling, health checks, and graceful shutdown handling.

use_cases.title: Event-Driven Processing
use_cases.description: Create Cloud Run Functions triggered by Pub/Sub messages or Cloud Storage uploads for asynchronous data processing workflows.

use_cases.title: Cost-Optimized Services
use_cases.description: Configure Cloud Run with right-sized resources, minimum instances, and CPU boost for latency-sensitive applications with budget constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Cloud Run Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy a Node.js Express application to Google Cloud Run. I have a Dockerfile ready. Guide me through the gcloud commands to build, push, and deploy the container with appropriate memory and CPU settings.

prompt_templates.title: Cloud Run Function for File Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Cloud Run Function that triggers when a file is uploaded to a Cloud Storage bucket. The function should process CSV files and load the data into BigQuery. Include error handling and logging.

prompt_templates.title: Cold Start Optimization Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: My Cloud Run service has 2-3 second cold start latency affecting user experience. Analyze my deployment configuration and recommend specific settings for minimum instances, CPU boost, memory, and code-level optimizations to reduce cold starts.

prompt_templates.title: Production-Ready CI/CD Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Cloud Build pipeline that automatically deploys my application to Cloud Run on every git push to main. Include multi-stage Docker builds, security scanning, staging deployment with manual approval, and production rollout with traffic splitting.

output_examples.input: Deploy a containerized API to Cloud Run with 512Mi memory and 1 CPU
output_examples.output: gcloud run deploy my-api --image gcr.io/project-id/my-api:v1 --region us-central1 --platform managed --memory 512Mi --cpu 1 --min-instances 0 --max-instances 100 --allow-unauthenticated

output_examples.input: Optimize Cloud Run service for low-latency responses
output_examples.output: Applied cold start optimizations: enabled CPU boost, set min-instances to 1, increased memory to 1Gi for faster startup, implemented lazy loading for BigQuery client, and configured concurrency to 80 for balanced throughput.

best_practices: Use multi-stage Docker builds with distroless base images to reduce container size and attack surface
Set appropriate concurrency values based on your application's memory and CPU requirements
Implement health check endpoints and graceful shutdown handlers for reliable deployments
anti_patterns: Running CPU-intensive work without setting concurrency to 1, which starves other requests
Writing large files to the /tmp directory which consumes memory and causes OOM errors
Starting background tasks that continue after response is sent, as CPU is throttled when idle

faq.question: What is the difference between Cloud Run and Cloud Functions?
faq.answer: Cloud Run runs containerized services with any runtime and is better for complex applications. Cloud Functions is for simple event-driven code without container management. Cloud Run offers more flexibility; Cloud Functions offers simpler deployment.

faq.question: How do I reduce cold start latency on Cloud Run?
faq.answer: Enable CPU boost, set minimum instances to 1 or higher, increase memory allocation for more CPU during startup, use smaller container images, and implement lazy initialization for heavy dependencies.

faq.question: Can Cloud Run connect to databases?
faq.answer: Yes, Cloud Run can connect to Cloud SQL using the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar or direct connection with IAM authentication. For other databases, use VPC connectors to access resources in your VPC network.

faq.question: What is the maximum request timeout for Cloud Run?
faq.answer: Cloud Run has a maximum request timeout of 60 minutes. For longer-running tasks, use Cloud Run with asynchronous processing patterns or consider Cloud Tasks for job queue management.

faq.question: How does Cloud Run pricing work?
faq.answer: You pay for vCPU and memory allocated during request processing, billed per 100ms. With minimum instances set, you pay for idle capacity. CPU is only allocated during requests unless you enable CPU always allocated.

faq.question: Can I run scheduled jobs on Cloud Run?
faq.answer: Yes, use Cloud Scheduler to trigger HTTP endpoints on Cloud Run on a schedule. Alternatively, use Pub/Sub with scheduled topics to trigger Cloud Run Functions for event-driven scheduled tasks.
